Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - The MDA 1977 Framework
The Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 is the foundational statute for controlled drugs in Ireland. It has been amended
substantially (Misuse of Drugs Acts 1984, 1996, 2007, 2016, and 2024 amendments). This chapter establishes
the framework concepts that the subsequent offence chapters apply.

1.1 Controlled Drug - The Statutory Concept
A 'controlled drug' for MDA purposes is any substance, product or preparation specified in the Schedule to the
Act (as updated by Regulations). The Schedule lists substances by chemical name and includes hundreds of
named substances. The principal operational categories:
Opioids - heroin (diamorphine), morphine, methadone, fentanyl analogues.
Cocaine and stimulants - cocaine HCl, crack cocaine, amphetamines, methamphetamine.
Cannabis - cannabis resin, herbal cannabis, cannabinoids.
Synthetics - MDMA, LSD, ketamine, GHB.
Sedatives - benzodiazepines (named varieties).
Other - anabolic steroids, prepared opium, etc.

1.2 Class A / B / C - Sentencing Significance
Drugs are categorised Class A, B, or C in the Misuse of Drugs Regulations. The classification drives the
sentencing range:

1.3 Possession - The Foundational Concept
'Possession' for MDA purposes has both factual and legal dimensions:
Physical custody - the drug is on the person or in their immediate control.
Constructive possession - the drug is in a place under the person's control (a vehicle they own, a room they
exclusively occupy) and they have knowledge of its presence.
Joint possession - multiple persons can have possession of the same drugs where each knows and consents.

1.3.1 The Knowledge Element
Possession requires knowledge - the accused must know they have the substance. Knowledge is typically
inferred from circumstances: where the drug was found, in what condition, the suspect's reaction. Defences of
'no knowledge' / 'planted' / 'someone else's drugs' engage the knowledge element.

1.3.2 The Identity-of-Substance Element
The accused must know that the substance is a controlled drug. Knowledge of the specific class is not required
(the prosecution does not need to prove the accused knew it was Class A vs Class B), but knowledge that it is
a controlled drug is required. A person who believed they had a non-controlled substance has a possible defence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.4 The Schedule and the Regulations
The current scheduling is set out in the Misuse of Drugs Regulations 2017 (and subsequent amending SIs).
New psychoactive substances are scheduled by Order following emergence. Before relying on a substance's
classification, check the current consolidated Schedule. The FSI report on any seizure confirms the substance
and its classification at the time of analysis.

1.5 Prescribed Drugs and Defences
Many controlled drugs are also prescribed medicines (e.g., diazepam, methadone). The MDA framework
permits possession of prescribed drugs by the prescribed person within the prescription. Defences engage
where:
The accused has a valid prescription for the substance.
The quantity matches the prescription.
Period of possession matches prescription duration.
Verification: ask the suspect to nominate their GP / prescriber; verify by phone or written check. The presence
of original-pharmacy packaging supports a prescription claim; loose tablets in unlabelled containers do not.

1.6 Specific Statutory Definitions
Cannabis: The flowering or fruiting tops of the plant cannabis sativa; cannabis resin (hashish, charas);
cannabinoid extracts (concentrates, oils, edibles where containing controlled cannabinoid).
Sale or supply: Includes giving, exchanging, transferring, distributing - whether for value or not. Free supply
to a friend is supply.
Production: Manufacture, cultivation, preparation (cannabis cultivation is also covered specifically under
s.17).
Importation: Bringing into the State (Customs and Excise framework operates alongside).

1.7 The Cautioning Scheme (Cannabis)
Operational policy has historically permitted diversion of first-offence simple cannabis possession through an
Adult Cautioning Scheme. The policy framework is set by Garda HQ / Department of Justice and is subject to
change - verify current scheme status before disposal decisions. Where the scheme operates: first-offence
simple cannabis possession may be disposed by Adult Caution rather than charge, subject to suspect
cooperation, no prior cautions / convictions, and other criteria. The scheme does not extend to Class A or other
classes.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Possession - s.3 MDA 1977
s.3 MDA 1977
Where the prohibition is breached, the offence is committed. Penalties vary by class and trial venue.

Legal Framework
Statute: s.3 Misuse of Drugs Act 1977.
Elements: (a) controlled drug; (b) in the possession of the accused; (c) without lawful authority (e.g., valid
prescription).
Court: Summary or indictable depending on class and circumstances.
Penalty (Class A): Summary - 12 months / fine; Indictment - up to 7 years.
Penalty (Class B): Summary - 12 months / fine; Indictment - up to 5 years.
Penalty (Class C): Summary - 12 months / fine; Indictment - up to 2 years.
Power of arrest: s.24 MDA - arrestable offence where indictable form engaged.
Diversion: Adult Cautioning Scheme for first-offence simple cannabis (subject to current policy).

Track A - Live Incident Response
Lawful basis for stop / search (s.23 MDA - reasonable cause); or arrest for other offence with consequential
search; or warrant execution.
Find of suspected controlled drug - secure immediately.
Field-test indication where appropriate (recognising field tests are presumptive only - FSI confirmation
needed).
Caution where investigation crystallises against the suspect.
Arrest under s.24 if circumstances require / indictable form engaged.

Track B - Post-Arrest Processing
Exhibit handling - immediate sealing, labelling, custody record.
Custody - book in; member-in-charge entries; medical assessment if suspect intoxicated.
FSI submission - sample submitted for analysis with appropriate documentation.
Interview - PEACE structure; possession, knowledge, identity-of-substance.
Charge sheet or summons; bail considerations.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words (s.3)
'[Name], I am arresting you under section 24 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 for an offence contrary to section
3 of the same Act. You have a controlled drug, namely [substance], in your possession. You are not obliged to
say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given
in evidence.'
Notebook - Critical Entries
Lawful basis of stop / search (s.23 cause OR warrant OR consequential).
Location, time, parties present.
Specific find - where on person / in vehicle / in premises.
Quantity (initial visual / weight assessment).
Suspect response on finding / arrest.
Exhibit number and sealing.
Custody handover time.

Interview Plan - s.3
Identification.
Confirm presence / discovery location.
Knowledge of the substance - did the suspect know it was there.
Identity of substance - did the suspect know it was a controlled drug.
Ownership - whose drugs are they.
Source - where did the suspect get them (relevant context, not always answered).
Purpose - personal use / supply (informs whether to consider s.15 charge).
Prescription - any valid prescription claim.
Anticipate defences: no knowledge; planted by another; not own drugs.

PULSE / File Index - s.3
PULSE incident - controlled drug / s.3.
Notebook entries (member, any colleague).
BWV where activated (stop and search context).
Exhibit register entries (each step in chain).
FSI report (analysis of substance and weight).
Custody record.
Interview transcript.
Charge sheet / summons.
Prior PULSE for prior dispositions (cautions, convictions).

Charge Selection

Specimen Wording - Statement of Member
'At [time] on [date] at [location], a public place, I observed [name] acting in a manner I considered suspicious
- specifically [behaviour]. I formed reasonable cause to suspect [name] was in possession of controlled drugs.
I identified myself, explained the search power under s.23 MDA 1977, and conducted a search of [name]'s
person. In the right-hand pocket of his jacket I located [item description - e.g., a small clear plastic bag
containing a quantity of brown powdered substance suspected to be heroin]. I cautioned [name] who replied
[reply]. I arrested [name] under s.24 MDA 1977 for an offence under s.3 MDA 1977. The substance was
secured, labelled exhibit [number], and the chain of custody commenced. The substance was subsequently
submitted to FSI for analysis (FSI report at [reference]).'

Defence Will Attack
Search unlawful - s.23 cause not made out. Rebut by specific articulable cause in notebook (smell, conduct,
intelligence specifics, observation).
Not the suspect's substance - someone else's; planted. Rebut by recovery location (pocket, immediate control),
fingerprints / DNA on packaging if appropriate, suspect's reaction.
No knowledge of substance presence. Rebut by location, suspect's awareness on questioning, conduct.
Not aware it was a controlled drug - believed it was a different substance. Rebut by FSI identification,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

packaging, context.
Valid prescription. Rebut by verifying with prescriber; prescription quantity / period.
Casey / identification - rarely engaged in possession found-with cases but may arise where drugs found in
vehicle / premises with multiple parties.
JC exclusion - procedural breach in search / warrant / detention. Rebut by procedural rigour at every step.

Red Flag Escalation
Quantity escalating possession to supply (s.15) or s.15A.
Suspect indicators of trafficking - cash, multiple phones, scales, packaging materials.
Vulnerable suspect - minor, person in addiction crisis, mental health concern - welfare disposal considerations.
Other offences engaged (firearms, organised crime, sex offences).
Suspect's behaviour suggesting medical emergency (overdose risk) - medical first.

Common Pitfalls
Inadequate articulation of s.23 reasonable cause in notebook.
Exhibit chain gaps - lost time, missed signatures, inadequate sealing.
FSI submission delay - timeliness affects evidential value and case progression.
Interview conducted with intoxicated suspect (voluntariness challenge under Madden).
Failure to consider Adult Caution diversion where eligible.
Misidentification of substance class (presumed Class A but FSI identifies otherwise) - impacts charge selection.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 - proportionate use of search power.
Standard 5 - exhibit and statement integrity.
Standard 6 - impartial stop-and-search judgment.
Welfare - addiction / vulnerability considerations alongside enforcement.

Compliment / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where Garda formed clean s.23 cause, conducted professional search, secured exhibit with
rigorous chain, and disposed appropriately (charge or caution depending on circumstances).
Cross-reference: Ch.3 (s.15 supply); Ch.4 (s.15A value); Ch.7 (search powers); Statutory Anchors; Case Law
(Damache, JC).

Chapter 3 - Possession for Sale or Supply - s.15 MDA 1977
s.15(1) MDA 1977

3.1 Legal Framework
Statute: s.15 Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 (as amended).
Elements: (a) controlled drug in possession; (b) intent to sell or otherwise supply; (c) supply would be in
contravention of regulations (i.e., not authorised - e.g., not a licensed pharmacist supplying on prescription).
Court: Indictable; can be tried summarily for lower-end cases.
Penalty (Class A): Up to life imprisonment on indictment; 12 months / fine summary.
Penalty (Class B): Up to 14 years on indictment.
Penalty (Class C): Up to 5 years on indictment.
Power of arrest: Arrestable offence - s.4 CJA 1984 detention applies; Drug Trafficking Act 1996 s.2 detention
up to 7 days for serious cases.

3.2 The Intent Element - Inferring Intent to Supply
The distinguishing feature of s.15 from s.3 is the intent to supply. Intent is rarely directly stated - it is inferred
from circumstances. The recognised supply-indicators:
Quantity beyond plausible personal use (drug-specific; FSI / experienced Garda evidence supports the analysis).
Packaging - multiple individual deals, scales for weighing, bulk packaging.
Paraphernalia of supply - cutting agents, plastic bags, weighing scales, deal lists.
Cash - significant amounts inconsistent with declared income; high-denomination notes; rolled bundles.
Multiple mobile phones - particularly 'burner' phones for dealing.
Premises features - dealing observations, traffic patterns, surveillance.
Suspect's lifestyle - inconsistent with declared income; high-value items.
Communications - text messages indicating supply transactions, customer lists.
Garda observation of supply transactions (street level).
Surveillance evidence - if specialist operation.

3.3 Track A - Live Incident Response
Securing scene - the find of drugs in supply-indicator context.
Securing exhibits - drugs, paraphernalia, cash, phones, packaging - each separately and labelled.
Securing premises (if relevant) - preserve for search.
Securing the suspect - arrest under s.24 MDA or s.4 CJA 1984.
BWV where activated.
Risk assessment - associates, weapons, organised crime indicators.

3.4 Track B - Post-Incident Investigation
3.4.1 Exhibit Strategy
Drugs - FSI for analysis (substance and weight).
Cash - photographed, counted in pairs (one Garda counts, one verifies), notes seal as exhibit; CAB referral
consideration.
Phones - secured for forensic examination (Garda Cyber Crime Bureau or specialist); examined under warrant.
Scales / packaging - fingerprints / DNA where relevant; weighing confirmation; pattern of use.
Documents - dealing lists, contact lists, records of transactions.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.4.2 Forensic Strategy
FSI analysis of drugs - composition, purity, weight.
Fingerprints and DNA on packaging - link to suspect.
Phone forensic examination - messages, contacts, location data.
Premises forensic examination if warrant executed.

3.4.3 Financial Strategy
Cash seizure - documented, secured, where lawful basis.
Account inquiry - via lawful framework (court order, suspect consent, statutory provision).
Lifestyle inquiry - vehicles, property, holidays - inconsistency with declared income.
CAB referral where appropriate (Volume 5 has CAB / POC appendix; the criminal investigation works
alongside the civil proceeds-of-crime framework).

3.5 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
3.5.1 Specimen Arrest Words (s.15)
'[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 15 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977. You have a
controlled drug, namely [substance], in your possession with intent to sell or otherwise supply it in
contravention of regulations. You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do
say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.'

3.5.2 Notebook Critical Entries
All elements as for s.3 plus the supply-indicators specifically:
Quantity and packaging description.
Cash with denomination breakdown.
Phones - quantity, type, location.
Scales / paraphernalia.
Premises observations (if applicable).
Suspect's lifestyle indicators (where evident).

3.6 Interview Plan - s.15
Identification and presence.
Drugs - possession, knowledge, identity-of-substance (as for s.3).
Intent - personal use vs supply. The suspect's account on this matters.
Source - where did the drugs come from. Often answered 'won't say' - inference may engage.
Cash - source / purpose / ownership.
Phones - whose, why multiple.
Scales / paraphernalia - purpose.
Premises (if relevant) - who occupies, control, knowledge.
Anticipate defences: personal use (not supply); not own drugs; not own cash; phones for legitimate reasons.
Inferences from silence (s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984) may engage with Buck compliance.

3.7 PULSE / File Index - s.15
PULSE incident - drug trafficking / s.15.
Member statements (initial responders, investigator).
BWV from initial encounter.
Surveillance / observation evidence (if any).
Exhibit register - drugs, cash, phones, scales, paraphernalia, documents.

FSI report on drugs.
Phone forensic examination report.
Fingerprint / DNA reports.
Premises search warrant material (Damache compliance).
CAB referral documentation (where applicable).
Custody record, interview transcript.
Charge sheet.
Prior antecedents (PULSE).

3.9 Specimen Wording - Statement of Member
'At [time] on [date], following the lawful arrest of [name] at [location], my colleague Garda [name] and I
conducted a search of his person and his vehicle (registration [number]) under the powers conferred by [s.23
MDA 1977 / s.24 search incidental]. The search yielded: [list with exhibit numbers - drugs, weight estimate,
packaging; cash and amount; phones and number; scales; etc.]. The quantity of [substance] (initial weight
estimate [grams]) significantly exceeds what would be consistent with personal use. The presence of
[scales/packaging/cash] in combination with the quantity supports the inference of intent to supply. I formed
the view that [name] was in possession of the controlled drug with intent to sell or otherwise supply it in
contravention of regulations. I cautioned [name] and arrested him for an offence under s.15 MDA 1977
[details].'

3.10 Defence Will Attack
Personal use - the quantity / paraphernalia consistent with personal use. Rebut by experienced Garda evidence
/ FSI analysis on quantity / context evidence.
Not own drugs - in vehicle / premises used by others. Rebut by constructive possession analysis, fingerprints /
DNA, suspect's reaction.
Cash not drug-related - legitimate source. Rebut by lifestyle analysis, bank records, employment income,
contradictions.
Phones legitimate - work, personal. Rebut by content (messages indicating dealing), pattern of use, multiplicity.
Search / arrest unlawful - JC challenge. Rebut by procedural rigour.
Casey / identification - if relevant.

3.11 Red Flag Escalation
Value EUR 13,000+ - s.15A engaged.
Firearms / weapons - additional offences and serious threat profile.
Organised crime indicators - sustained operation; intelligence-led.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Cross-border / importation indicators - Customs joint operation.
Money laundering indicators - CAB / Financial Intelligence Unit engagement.
Vulnerable persons exploited (minors, persons in addiction).

3.12 Common Pitfalls
Inadequate articulation of intent-to-supply indicators - the file should set them out specifically.
Failing to seize cash / phones / paraphernalia as exhibits at scene.
Phone forensic delays affecting case progression.
Failure to consider s.15A where value approaches threshold.
CAB referral missed where lifestyle / asset profile warrants.
Search powers misapplied - s.23 vs warrant vs s.24 arrest-incidental.

3.13 Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 5 - exhibit and evidence integrity at every step.
Standard 6 - same threshold across communities and demographics.
Standard 4 - force in arrest proportionate.

3.14 Compliment Points / Cross-References
Strong s.15 case-build is sustained investigative work - intelligence, observation, search, forensic, financial and is recognised at the formal commendation level where the operation is significant.
Cross-reference: Ch.2 (s.3 distinction); Ch.4 (s.15A escalation); Ch.7 (powers); CAB / POC appendix; Case
Law (sentencing line).

Chapter 4 - s.15A - Value EUR 13,000+ Presumptive
Sentencing
s.15A MDA 1977 (inserted by s.4 CJA 1999)

4.1 Significance of s.15A
s.15A was introduced by the Criminal Justice Act 1999 to provide a serious-supply offence with a presumptive
10-year minimum sentence on indictment. It targets the more serious end of the drug trafficking spectrum. Two
key operational dimensions:
The EUR 13,000 market-value threshold is the entry to the offence - below threshold is s.15.
The 10-year presumptive sentence engages unless the court is satisfied that exceptional and specific
circumstances would render it unjust (s.27(3D), (3F)).

4.2 Legal Framework
Statute: s.15A MDA 1977 (as inserted by s.4 CJA 1999 and amended).
Elements: (a) controlled drug(s) in possession; (b) intent to sell or otherwise supply in contravention of
regulations; (c) market value EUR 13,000 or more (single or aggregate).
Court: Indictable only - Circuit Criminal Court.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment; presumptive minimum 10 years under s.27(3C) MDA.
Detention: Drug Trafficking Act 1996 s.2 - up to 7 days detention in stages.
Arrest: s.24 MDA arrestable offence.

4.3 The Market Value Test
Market value is the value the drug or drugs would have if sold in the State at the relevant time. Determination
involves:
FSI weighs and identifies the substance (composition, purity).
Garda market value evidence (specialised Garda witnesses provide market value evidence at trial).
Per-gram / per-unit pricing for the substance based on contemporary market data.
Aggregation across multiple substances where applicable.

4.3.1 Market Value Evidence at Trial
Trained Gardai (typically with specialist drugs unit experience) give evidence of market value at trial. The
evidence is opinion based on training, current market knowledge, and the specific drug analysed. Build the case
so that the market value evidence supports the threshold.

4.4 The Presumptive Sentence Regime
s.27(3C) MDA 1977

4.4.1 The Exception - s.27(3D), (3F)
The 10-year minimum applies unless the court is satisfied that there are exceptional and specific circumstances
which would render the imposition of a sentence of not less than 10 years unjust. The factors the court may
consider in determining whether exceptional circumstances exist include:
Whether the accused pleaded guilty and at what stage.
Material assistance to investigation.
Whether the accused was a courier or had a more substantial role.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The accused's circumstances (age, addiction, family responsibilities, prior record).
The nature and quantity of the drug.
The actual or potential consequences of the supply.

4.4.2 The Sentencing Jurisprudence
The Irish Court of Appeal / Court of Criminal Appeal has developed an extensive jurisprudence on s.15A
sentencing. Key cases include:
DPP v Renald [2001] - early authority on the presumptive regime.
DPP v Ducque [2005] - exceptional circumstances framework.
DPP v Long [2009] / DPP v Anderson - presumption mechanics.
Modern appellate practice on the structured analysis.
See the Case Law Appendix for vLex-depth treatment of the principal authorities.

4.5 Track A - Live Incident Response
s.15A cases typically arise from one of three operational scenarios:
Securing the scene - drugs, cash, phones, premises occupants.
Exhibit handling - rigorous from the moment of seizure given the case importance.
Arrest of suspect under s.24 MDA / Drug Trafficking Act detention.
Specialist support - Garda Drugs Unit / Drugs and Organised Crime Bureau (DOCB) typically engaged in major
seizures.

4.6 Track B - Investigation
4.6.1 Exhibit and Forensic Strategy
FSI urgent / priority submission given case significance.
Photography of seizure with scale, in context, before disturbance.
Counting / weighing protocol - by paired Gardai, recorded.
Fingerprint and DNA strategy on packaging (every layer).
Phone forensic - urgent given communications evidence likely critical.
CCTV recovery from any relevant location.

4.6.2 Surveillance / Intelligence-Led Operation
Surveillance log - times, observations, identifications.
Source-handling material - via specialist framework, with disclosure considerations.
Coordination across teams - briefing material, command structure.

4.6.3 Communications Data
Phone data - call records, messages, contact lists, location data.
Communications Data access under the statutory framework (Communications Retention of Data Acts).

4.6.4 Financial Investigation
Cash seizure with chain documented.
Bank account inquiry where lawful.
Lifestyle assessment.
CAB referral - in most s.15A cases, CAB referral is appropriate to engage civil proceeds-of-crime framework
alongside criminal prosecution.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

4.7 Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
4.7.1 Specimen Arrest Words
'[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 15A of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977. You have in
your possession one or more controlled drugs, namely [substance], for the purpose of selling or otherwise
supplying them in contravention of regulations, the aggregate market value of which is EUR 13,000 or more.
You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in
writing and may be given in evidence.'

The 7-day detention regime is a powerful investigative tool. It must be exercised with proper authority at each

4.8 Interview Plan - s.15A
Identification.
Possession - link to the drugs / cash / phones / premises.
Knowledge - did the suspect know what they had.
Intent to supply - the quantity makes personal use claim implausible; the prosecution case is built on objective
indicators.
Role - courier, holder, organiser, supplier.
Source - upstream.
Distribution - downstream.
Co-conspirators - who else involved.
Communications - put extracted messages to suspect.
Financial picture - cash sources, lifestyle inconsistencies.
Anticipate defences: not own drugs (courier without knowledge); planted; valuation challenge (below EUR
13,000).
Inferences from silence (s.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984) - Buck compliance critical given case stakes.

4.9 PULSE / File Index - s.15A
S.15A files are major. The file structure:

4.10 Defences Will Attack
Below threshold - market valuation challenged. Rebut by specialist Garda market-value evidence + FSI

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

weight/purity.
Not own drugs / courier without knowledge. Rebut by indicators of knowledge (handling, packaging awareness,
communications), forensic links.
Planted by Gardai - challenges to chain of custody. Rebut by procedural rigour at every step.
Procedural challenge - warrant validity (Damache); detention framework compliance; interview procedural
compliance (Gormley, Buck).
Casey / identification - if relevant in surveillance cases.
Exceptional circumstances on sentencing - role, plea, assistance, circumstances. The investigation file informs
the sentencing court's view.

4.11 Red Flag Escalation
International dimension - importation considerations (s.27 MDA + Customs).
Firearms / weapons - additional serious offences.
Organised crime context - intelligence linkage to broader operations.
Vulnerable persons exploited (couriers under duress, vulnerable persons holding for others).
Multiple s.15A counts - aggregate impact.
Money laundering significant scale.

4.12 Common Pitfalls
Market value evidence not properly built - the threshold is constitutive of the offence.
Exhibit chain weak across the seizure-handling-FSI-court path.
Damache compliance issues in warrants.
Extended detention framework non-compliance.
Phone forensic timing slipping the case.
CAB referral missed where the asset profile clearly warrants.
Co-accused investigation not individualised - each accused needs separate evidence.

4.13 Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 5 - the case integrity is the prosecution's foundation.
Standard 4 - force in arrest / operation proportionate, with command-level authorisation.
Standard 6 - same procedural standards across cases regardless of suspect's profile.

4.14 Compliment / Cross-References
s.15A cases are formal commendation level work where major seizures are achieved through sustained
investigative engagement.
Cross-reference: Ch.3 (s.15 framework); Ch.5 (importation/production); Ch.7 (powers including 7-day
detention); CAB/POC Appendix; Case Law Appendix for sentencing line.

Chapter 5 - Production, Cultivation, Importation
5.1 Production / Manufacture - s.21 MDA 1977
s.21(1)(a) MDA

Legal Framework
Elements: (a) production of a controlled drug; (b) otherwise than under authorising regulations.
Production: Includes manufacturing, preparing, refining, and converting. Synthesis from precursor chemicals
is production.
Court: Indictable; serious offence.
Penalty: Up to life on indictment for Class A; substantial sentences for other classes.

Operational Application
Methamphetamine / amphetamine lab discovery - specialist hazard response, premises sealed pending forensic
assessment.
Cocaine 'cutting' operations - addition of cutting agents to imported product is production.
MDMA / ecstasy production - chemistry-based investigation; expert evidence.
Cannabis processing into resin - production (alongside cultivation s.17).

Operational Considerations
Production sites are hazardous - chemicals, fumes, flammable materials. Specialist (HazMat / chemical)
support.
Forensic chemistry expert engagement - process / yield analysis.
Precursor chemicals - regulation under the Misuse of Drugs Regulations and EU Precursor Regulations.
CAB / financial investigation - production operations often well-capitalised.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

5.2 Cultivation of Cannabis - s.17 MDA 1977
s.17 MDA

Legal Framework
Elements: (a) cultivation of cannabis plants; (b) without a licence.
Cultivation: Includes planting, growing, harvesting, processing while still in plant form.
Court: Indictable; can be summary for lower-scale.
Penalty: Up to 14 years on indictment.

Cultivation Operations - Operational Categories
Domestic small-scale - a few plants for personal use - usually lower-end disposal.
Indoor 'grow houses' - commercial scale; lighting, hydroponics, ventilation systems.
Large commercial operations - multi-room or industrial scale.

Investigation
Premises search warrant (Damache compliant) for indoor grows.
Electricity bypass detection - grow operations often have illegal electricity tapping (additional offences under
Electricity (Supply) Act framework).
Specialist horticultural evidence on yield, value, scale.
Plant counts and per-plant value (cumulative for s.15 / s.15A overlap analysis).
Identification of cultivators / those in charge of premises.

Charge Selection
Scale

5.3 Importation - s.27 MDA / Customs Acts
Importation of controlled drugs engages both the MDA 1977 framework and the Customs Acts. Operational
realities:
Customs - typically the lead seizing authority at ports / airports for unaccompanied or in-transit drugs.
Gardai - typically the lead investigative authority for the criminal investigation, with Customs / Revenue
cooperation.
Joint operations - Customs / Garda Drugs and Organised Crime Bureau (DOCB) coordinate on major
importations.
International cooperation - Europol, Interpol, foreign agencies.

Penalty
Up to life imprisonment on indictment for serious importation. s.15A may also engage where the imported
quantity exceeds the threshold.

Investigation
Importation route analysis - origin, transit, destination.
Concealment analysis - vehicle, person, container, postal.
Surveillance / controlled delivery (where lawfully structured).
Communications evidence with overseas elements.
Financial flows - international transfers, hawala, crypto.
Forensic chemistry - origin profiling of substances.

Exportation
S.28 MDA covers exportation. Less common operationally in Ireland (typically Ireland is import destination);
engages where Irish-produced controlled substances are exported or where Ireland is transit.

Chapter 7 - Drug Investigation Powers
This chapter consolidates the powers that underpin drug investigation: stop and search of persons (s.23 MDA);
arrest (s.24 MDA); premises search warrant (s.26 MDA); and the extended detention regime under the Criminal
Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996. Each power has procedural requirements that, if not met, undermine the
entire case under JC.

7.1 Stop and Search of Persons - s.23 MDA 1977
s.23(1) MDA

Legal Framework
Power: Search of person, vehicle, or thing; with reasonable cause to suspect possession in contravention of
MDA.
Reasonable cause: Specific, articulable, based on facts known to the member - smell, behaviour, intelligence,
conduct, location, etc.
Detention: Brief detention for the purpose of the search permitted.
Identification: Member should identify as a Garda; explain the power and reason for search where practicable.

The Reasonable Cause Standard
Reasonable cause under s.23 is the foundational requirement. The Garda must be able to articulate (a) what was
observed / known and (b) the inference of possession that follows. Examples of cause:
Strong smell of cannabis from person / vehicle.
Observation of behaviour consistent with drug transaction (handshake exchange, observed delivery).
Specific intelligence concerning the person.
Observed handling / hiding / dropping of suspected drug item.
Location (drug market area) combined with behaviour.
Visible drug packaging / paraphernalia.
Suspect's response to Garda presence (sudden movement, attempts to dispose).
Reasonable Cause - Not 'Hunch'
Reasonable cause is more than suspicion as a hunch or feeling. The member must be able to articulate the specific
facts that led to the inference of possession. Generic descriptions ('looked suspicious', 'known to police') without
specific reasons do not constitute reasonable cause. JC exclusion may follow where the threshold is not met.
Document the specific factual basis in the notebook contemporaneously.

Search Conduct
Identify yourself as a Garda.
Explain the power being exercised (s.23 MDA).
Explain the reason for the search.
Search professionally - dignity preserved, minimum invasiveness consistent with purpose.
Female suspect searched by female Garda where practicable.
Strip search - only at Garda station and with member-in-charge authorisation, separately recorded.
Document everything - what searched, what found, what said.

Common Issues
Inadequate cause - the operational pressure to find should not lower the threshold.
Public place vs private - s.23 power applies; entry to private premises requires warrant (s.26) or other power.
Detention period - 'reasonably necessary' - brief; if extended detention needed, arrest required.
Documentation - contemporaneous notebook entry with the specific cause.

7.3 Premises Search Warrant - s.26 MDA 1977
s.26 MDA (paraphrased)
[Important: s.26 has been the subject of constitutional analysis post-Damache - check current statutory text and
authority structure for warrant issuance.]

Damache Compliance
Following Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11, search warrants for dwellings must be issued by an independent
authority (typically District Court judge). Where s.26 historically permitted senior Garda issuance of warrants
in respect of premises, the framework has been adjusted by subsequent legislation and judicial guidance to
ensure constitutional compliance.

Operational Application
Apply via District Court for warrant where time permits.
Application on sworn information from Garda - specifying premises, basis for reasonable suspicion, scope of
search.
Warrant in writing, signed by issuing authority, time-limited (typically 7 days).
Execute warrant within validity period.
On execution: identify Gardai; produce warrant; explain power; search; seize; provide receipt for items seized.
Document execution thoroughly - times, occupants, items seized, condition of premises.

Search Execution Best Practice
Briefing before execution - Gardai know who they are, what they are looking for, the layout.
Safe entry - assess threats; force as necessary; PSU / specialist support where appropriate.
Systematic search - room by room, item by item, with exhibit officer recording.
Photography of premises before / during / after.
Each occupant addressed - identity, presence, knowledge.
Children present - welfare engagement (Tusla).

Investigative Use
The extended detention is designed to support complex investigation. Use it for:
Phone forensic examination.
Multi-suspect investigation - other suspects interviewed during the same period.
Search of additional premises following intelligence developments.
Forensic analysis (FSI urgent submission).
Financial investigation.
International inquiries (where time-zone differences require).
Do not use it as a passive holding period. The detention extension justification at each stage is what investigative

7.5 Other Powers - Summary
7.5.1 Garda Common Law Search Incidental to Arrest
On arrest, Gardai have a common-law power to search the arrested person and immediate vicinity for evidence
of the offence. This operates alongside s.23 (where the arrest follows a s.23 search) and as the basis for
searching after non-MDA arrests where drugs are then found.

7.5.2 Customs Powers
Customs officers have separate statutory powers for search of persons / vehicles / containers at points of entry
/ exit and in connection with customs / revenue offences. Joint operations operate under combined frameworks.

7.5.3 Powers of Examination of Documents / Records
MDA framework includes powers for examination of records (e.g., pharmacy / prescribing records) on premises
that handle controlled drugs lawfully. Used in healthcare-context investigations.

7.5.4 Forensic Sampling - CJA 2007
CJA 2007 framework for forensic sample taking from suspects engages in drug investigation where DNA /
fingerprints / hair samples may link to packaging / scene. Operates under its own statutory regime.

Appendix - Defences, Burdens, Charge Selection, File
Craft (Drugs)
Burdens framework carries from Volume 2. Drug-specific considerations on charge selection, file craft, and

Part A - Drug-Specific Defences
A.1 No Knowledge of the Substance
Defence: did not know the substance was there (planted, someone else's, unaware). Rebut by location of find,
suspect reaction, fingerprints / DNA on packaging, communications evidence.

A.2 No Knowledge It Was a Controlled Drug
Defence: thought it was something else (legal substance, lookalike). Rebut by FSI identification, packaging,
context, suspect's reaction on discovery.

A.3 Valid Prescription
Defence: lawfully prescribed. Verify with prescriber; check quantity and period match prescription; originalpharmacy packaging supports.

A.4 Possession Was Personal Use Not Supply
Defence specific to s.15: the quantity / circumstances support personal use, not supply. Rebut by intent-tosupply indicators (quantity, packaging, cash, phones, paraphernalia, communications, observation).

A.5 Procedural Challenges (JC Path)
Drug cases routinely challenge: s.23 reasonable cause; arrest grounds (Walsh); warrant validity (Damache);
detention compliance (CJA 1984 / CJ(DT)A 1996); interview compliance (Gormley, Doyle, Buck); chain of
custody.

A.6 Below s.15A Threshold (Market Value Challenge)
Defence specific to s.15A: market valuation challenged - quantity, purity, current market rate. Rebut by trained
Garda market-value witness evidence + FSI weight/purity + current market data.

Appendix - Evidence and Admissibility (Drugs)
Chain of custody, FSI evidence, market value evidence, phone evidence, communications data - the specific
evidential framework for drug cases.

Part A - Chain of Custody - The Foundation
The chain from seizure to court production is the foundation of every drug prosecution. The standard:
Every transfer is a potential chain-of-custody challenge. Document every step.

Part B - Best Practice Standards
Exhibit labels - unique identifier, description, date, member, location of find.
Sealing - tamper-evident seals; signature across seal.
Photography - exhibit before disturbance; with scale; on labelled background.
Counting / weighing - by paired Gardai; recorded contemporaneously.
Secure storage - exhibit room access controlled; entries logged.

Part C - FSI Evidence
Forensic Science Ireland is the standard analytical body for drug substances. FSI reports establish identity,
weight, purity. The forensic chemist gives evidence at trial (or report admitted by consent). Considerations:
Submission timing - delays affect case progression; priority requests for urgent cases.
Composition of sample submitted - representative of seizure; weighed before submission and after return.
Purity analysis - relevant for sentencing (very pure substances aggravate; very impure may indicate dilution).
Sampling protocol - particularly important for large seizures where representative sampling is the methodology.

Part D - Market Value Evidence
For s.15A threshold, trained Gardai with specialist drugs unit experience give evidence on market value.
Operational standard:
Witness drawn from specialist unit (DOCB or Divisional Drugs).
Witness has current market knowledge - active engagement with the drug market intelligence.
Calculation methodology stated - per gram / per unit / per deal multiplied by quantity.
Time-of-offence valuation (not current market on different date).

Part E - Phone and Communications Evidence
Phone evidence is central in modern drug investigations:
Seizure - secure device immediately; document seizure circumstances; airplane mode / Faraday bag to prevent
remote wipe.
Forensic examination - Garda Cyber Crime Bureau or specialist forensic examiner.
Warrant for examination where appropriate.
Examination report - examiner statement on methodology and findings.
Specific evidence extracted - messages (with metadata), contacts, call records, location data, photos / videos,
app data.
Multiple phones - cross-link analysis; pattern of use; burner-phone identification.

Part F - Communications Data Access

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Beyond the phone itself, retained telecommunications data may be accessed under statutory framework:
Communications (Retention of Data) Acts 2011, 2022 - statutory access regime.
CJEU Tele2 / Watson jurisprudence and subsequent EU / Irish case law shape the access framework.
Independent authorisation typically required.
Data sought - call records, cell-site information, internet logs.
Statement from access process supporting evidential use.

Part G - Surveillance and Observation Evidence
Surveillance and observation is operationally important in drug investigation. Evidence framework:
Surveillance log - contemporaneous, detailed, signed.
Identification at distance - Casey warning may engage at trial.
Observation team - multiple members supporting the identification.
Photography / video where lawfully obtained.
Statutory framework for surveillance - the Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 governs covert surveillance
for serious crime investigation.

Part H - The Hearsay Risk in Drug Cases
Witnesses may be unwilling - drug-context witnesses (street observers, supply-chain elements) may be
intimidated or fearful. The s.16 CJA 2006 framework (treated in Volume 2 and Volume 4) may engage to admit
prior statements.

Appendix - CAB and Proceeds of Crime Interface
Drug investigation typically engages financial and asset dimensions. The Criminal Assets Bureau (CAB) is the
multi-agency body that pursues civil proceeds-of-crime recovery. This appendix sets out the operational
interface between Garda criminal investigation and CAB.

Part A - CAB Overview
Multi-agency body: Gardai, Revenue, Department of Social Protection, Department of Justice.
Civil framework - operates on balance of probabilities.
Principal statute - Proceeds of Crime Act 1996 (as amended by 2005 Act and subsequent).
Mandate - identify, freeze, and ultimately recover proceeds of criminal conduct.

Part B - The Proceeds of Crime Act Framework
S.2 - Interim Order - freezes assets believed to be proceeds of crime (balance of probabilities).
S.3 - Interlocutory Order - if no challenge / unsuccessful challenge within 21 days, continues the freeze for up
to 7 years.
S.4 - Disposal Order - after the 7-year period, assets transferred to State.

Part C - When to Refer to CAB
CAB referral is appropriate where the criminal investigation reveals indicators of unexplained wealth or
proceeds of criminal conduct:
Significant cash seized in drug investigation.
Property / vehicles / assets inconsistent with declared income.
Lifestyle profile suggesting income beyond legitimate sources.
Bank account balances disproportionate to income.
Investigation reveals laundering arrangements.

Part D - The Garda Investigation - CAB Interface
Criminal investigation continues - CAB is parallel, not substitution.
CAB engagement via local CAB liaison / via referral form.
Information sharing within multi-agency framework.
CAB may seek freeze orders during criminal investigation.
CAB proceedings can continue even where criminal proceedings do not / are unsuccessful (different standard
of proof).

Part E - Money Laundering Offences
Separate from the civil POC framework, the criminal money laundering offences:
Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010 - principal Irish statute.
Offences include handling, transferring, concealing the proceeds of criminal conduct with knowledge or
reasonable suspicion of their criminal origin.
Up to 14 years on indictment.
Garda Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) coordinates ML investigation.

Part A - Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 - Core Offences
A.1 s.3 - Possession
s.3(1): Subject to subsection (3) and section 4, a person shall not have a controlled drug in his possession.
Penalty: Class A: 12 months / fine summary; 7 yrs indictment. Class B: 12 months / fine; 5 yrs. Class C: 12
months / fine; 2 yrs.

A.2 s.15 - Possession for Sale or Supply
s.15(1): A person who has a controlled drug in his possession, whether lawfully or not, with intent to sell or
otherwise supply it to another in contravention of regulations under section 5, shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: Class A: 12 months / fine summary; life indictment. Class B: 14 yrs indictment. Class C: 5 yrs
indictment.

A.3 s.15A - Possession Value EUR 13,000+
s.15A(1): A person shall be guilty of an offence under this section where (a) the person has in his possession
one or more controlled drugs for the purpose of selling or otherwise supplying it or them to another in
contravention of regulations under section 5 of this Act, and (b) the market value of the controlled drug or the
aggregate of the market values of the controlled drugs, as the case may be, is EUR 13,000 or more.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment; presumptive minimum 10 yrs under s.27(3C).

A.4 s.15B - Supply to Prisoner
s.15B(1): A person who supplies or attempts to supply a controlled drug to a person in a prison shall be guilty
of an offence.
Penalty: Up to 10 yrs indictment; s.27(3C) presumptive 10-year regime applies.

A.5 s.15C - Supply to Person Under 17
s.15C: A person who supplies or attempts to supply a controlled drug to a person under 17 years shall be guilty
of an offence.
Penalty: Up to 5 yrs indictment.

A.6 s.17 - Cultivation
s.17: A person who cultivates any plant of the genus cannabis except in pursuance of a licence issued under
regulations made by the Minister shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: Up to 14 yrs indictment; 12 months / fine summary.

A.7 s.21 - Production
s.21(1)(a): A person shall not produce, otherwise than under and in accordance with regulations, a controlled
drug.
Penalty: Class A: life. Substantial penalties for other classes.

A.8 s.27 - Importation
s.27 framework: Importation of controlled drugs; offence and sentencing under MDA + Customs framework.
Penalty: Up to life imprisonment for serious importation.

Part B - MDA Powers
B.1 s.23 - Stop and Search
s.23(1): A member of the Garda Siochana who with reasonable cause suspects that a person is in possession in
contravention of this Act of a controlled drug may, without warrant, (a) search the person, and, if he or she
considers it necessary for that purpose, detain the person for such time as is reasonably necessary for that
purpose; (b) search any vehicle or any other thing, and, if he considers it necessary for that purpose, detain the
vehicle or other thing for such time as is reasonably necessary.

B.2 s.24 - Arrest
s.24: Without prejudice to any other powers conferred by law, a member of the Garda Siochana may arrest
without warrant any person whom the member with reasonable cause suspects to be committing or to have
committed an offence under this Act (subject to specified minor offence exclusions).

B.3 s.26 - Premises Search Warrant
Search warrant framework for premises. Operates on Damache-compliant independent authorisation.
Authorises entry, search of premises, seizure of controlled drugs and related items.

Part C - Sentencing Provisions
C.1 s.27(3C) - Presumptive 10-Year Minimum
s.27(3C): Where a person (a) is convicted of an offence under section 15A or 15B, and (b) the court is of the
opinion that the imposition of a sentence of not less than 10 years' imprisonment as the minimum term to be
served would be appropriate in all the circumstances, the court shall impose such a sentence.

C.2 s.27(3D), (3F) - Exception Framework
Exception where exceptional and specific circumstances would render the imposition of the 10-year minimum
unjust. Factors include plea, cooperation, role, personal circumstances, drug nature and quantity, actual or
potential consequences.

Part D - Adjacent Legislation
D.1 Criminal Justice Act 1984 - Custody
s.4 detention framework applies to MDA arrestable offences. 6 hrs initial + 6 hrs Superintendent + 12 hrs Chief
Superintendent = up to 24 hrs.

D.2 Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 - Extended Detention
s.2 framework for extended detention up to 7 days in stages for drug trafficking offences. Authorisations at
each stage as set out in Ch.7 of this volume.

D.3 Criminal Justice (Psychoactive Substances) Act 2010
Generic framework for psychoactive substances not specifically scheduled. Offences of sale, importation,
exportation, advertisement, supply. Possession by user not criminalised.

D.4 Proceeds of Crime Act 1996 (and 2005 amendment)
Civil proceeds of crime framework. s.2 Interim Order; s.3 Interlocutory Order; s.4 Disposal Order. CAB
operates under this framework.

D.5 Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010
Criminal money laundering offences. Up to 14 years indictment.

D.6 Communications (Retention of Data) Acts 2011, 2022
Communications data retention and access framework. Operative for drug investigation communications data

D.7 Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009
Covert surveillance framework for serious crime including drug trafficking.

D.8 Customs Acts
Customs framework operates alongside MDA on importation. Customs and Revenue powers for search at

Appendix - Case Law Resource (Vol 5 - Drugs)
Drug investigation engages two principal Irish case law strands: the procedural / evidential authorities that carry
from Volume 2 (Walsh, Madden, Casey, Buck, Gormley, Doyle, JC, Damache) and the drug-specific
sentencing line developed extensively by the Court of Criminal Appeal / Court of Appeal on s.15A presumptive
sentencing. This appendix treats each in vLex-style depth (Citation / Facts / Holding / Reasoning / Significance
/ Operational Application).
Source verification reminder
Drug sentencing case law continues to develop. Verify citations on vLex Ireland, BAILII, or the Courts Service
judgments database before relying. The s.15A presumptive regime has been clarified across many appellate
decisions; the structured approach is now well-established but specific sentence factors and exception cases
continue to refine.

Part A - Procedural Authorities Applied to Drug Investigation
A.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - Arrest Reasonable Suspicion
Operational Application (Drugs): Every drug arrest under s.24 MDA must rest on the arresting Garda's
articulable reasonable suspicion. Notebook capture: what did the Garda observe / know that grounded the
suspicion of MDA offence. For s.23 searches the same standard applies to 'reasonable cause' for the search.

A.2 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness
Operational Application (Drugs): Drug suspects in custody are frequently intoxicated, in withdrawal, or
under stress. Interview must be conducted when the suspect is fit. Voluntariness is challenged routinely in drug
cases - the medical assessment at custody and the interview-readiness judgment matter.

A.3 The People (DPP) v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 - Inference Prerequisites
Operational Application (Drugs): Inferences from silence under s.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984 are commonly
sought in drug investigations - re. source of drugs, presence at premises, ownership of items, contents of phones.
Buck-strict compliance: prescribed warning; pre-interview consultation; question within statutory scope;
recorded response.

A.4 The People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 - Identification
Operational Application (Drugs): Identification engages in surveillance / observation cases. Where the
prosecution depends on Garda observation of a specific person at a specific transaction, Casey applies. VIPER
and ADVOKATE assessment support contested identifications.

A.5 DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 - Pre-Interview Solicitor
Operational Application (Drugs): Carries fully. Drug suspects are often well-versed in custody rights; preinterview solicitor consultation is invariably requested and must be facilitated. The Gormley right cannot be
circumvented.

A.6 DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 - Interview Solicitor Presence
Operational Application (Drugs): Carries fully. Where the suspect requests solicitor presence in interview,
facilitate.

A.7 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 - Modern Exclusionary Rule
Operational Application (Drugs): Drug cases are routinely contested on procedural grounds - warrant
validity, s.23 cause adequacy, chain of custody, detention compliance. The JC inadvertence margin is narrow.
Comply rigorously.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

A.8 Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 - Warrant Independence
Citation: [2012] IESC 11 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Damache challenged the constitutionality of a search warrant issued by a Garda Superintendent under
s.29 Offences against the State Act 1939 for a search of his dwelling. The Superintendent was directing the
investigation. Argued the warrant breached constitutional protections of the dwelling under Article 40.5 by
reason of the absence of independent authorisation.
Holding: S.29 OAS Act 1939 unconstitutional insofar as it permitted Garda issue of warrant for the dwelling
by a member involved in the investigation. The constitutional protection of the dwelling requires independent
(judicial / quasi-judicial) authorisation.
Reasoning: Denham CJ framed the inviolability of the dwelling under Article 40.5 as requiring genuine
independent authorisation of intrusion. Self-authorisation by the investigation's own director did not meet the
constitutional standard.
Significance: All search warrant frameworks must be Damache-compliant. Where statutory provisions
permitted internal Garda authorisation of warrants for dwellings, those provisions were vulnerable to
constitutional challenge and have been adjusted.
Operational Application (Drugs): Search warrants for dwelling premises in drug investigations to District
Court. The s.26 MDA warrant framework operates on the Damache-compliant basis (independent authority).
Where the search is not of a dwelling (commercial premises, vehicle, etc.) different considerations may apply
but the safe approach is independent authorisation in all but the most operationally urgent (and otherwise
statutorily-authorised) situations. Where warrant is not used (e.g., search incident to arrest), the statutory basis
must be identified.

Part B - The s.15A Presumptive Sentencing Line
The Court of Criminal Appeal / Court of Appeal has developed an extensive jurisprudence on s.15A sentencing
since the offence was created by the CJA 1999. The line of authority addresses how the 10-year presumptive
sentence operates, what counts as 'exceptional and specific circumstances' under s.27(3D), and how the
structured discretion plays out in practice. The principal authorities:

B.1 The People (DPP) v Renald (unreported, Court of Criminal Appeal, 23 November
2001)
Facts: Early s.15A appellate consideration shortly after the regime came into force.
Holding: The 10-year presumptive minimum is not a 'mandatory' sentence. The court retains discretion to
depart from the minimum where the statutory exception is established. The exception is not narrowly confined
- the court conducts a structured analysis of the case.
Significance: Established the framework: presumption operates; departure available on exceptional and
specific circumstances; structured analysis required.
Operational Application: File preparation should support the sentencing exercise on the offender-specific
factors that may engage exception or aggravation.

B.2 The People (DPP) v Ducque [2005] IECCA 92
Citation: [2005] IECCA 92 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: Sentence appeal in a s.15A case. Court considered the framework for assessing exceptional and specific
circumstances.
Holding: Exceptional and specific circumstances are case-specific. The court considers: the role of the offender
(courier / facilitator / principal); plea (timing and value); cooperation (material assistance); personal
circumstances (addiction, family); the nature and quantity of drugs; aggravating or mitigating context.
Significance: Established the multi-factor framework for the exception analysis. The court's analysis is
structured but the determination is fact-sensitive.
Operational Application: Build the file so that the court has the material to conduct the Ducque analysis - the
offender's role and circumstances are documented in the antecedent statement and investigation file.

B.3 The People (DPP) v Long [2009] 3 IR 486
Citation: [2009] 3 IR 486; [2008] IECCA 133 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Holding: Refined the s.15A sentencing approach. The court should identify the appropriate sentence by
reference to the gravity of the offence (Tiernan structured discretion), and then apply the statutory minimum
unless exception established. Where exception established, the court determines the appropriate departure.
Significance: Brought the s.15A regime into the wider Tiernan structured discretion framework. The
presumption is integrated with the general sentencing analysis.
Operational Application: The file should support the Tiernan analysis (gravity, aggravation, mitigation) as
well as the s.15A specific factors. The two analyses operate together.

B.4 The People (DPP) v Anderson and Others [2010] - The Aggregation and Role Analysis
Significance: Court of Criminal Appeal authority on the operation of the aggregation rule (multiple substances
aggregated to threshold) and the assessment of the offender's role within the supply chain. Couriers / lower-tier
actors typically attract lower departures from the presumptive minimum than organisers / principals; the
structured analysis applies.
Operational Application: Document the offender's role specifically. Investigation evidence on whether the
accused was the source / organiser / mid-level / courier / vulnerable holder is operationally important - it directly
affects sentencing.

B.5 Modern Court of Appeal Authority on s.15A
The Court of Appeal (since 2014) has continued to develop the s.15A jurisprudence. The structured approach

is now well-established. Key contemporary themes:
Plea timing - early plea attracts more credit than late plea.
Material cooperation - genuine assistance to investigation can substantially reduce sentence.
Vulnerable couriers - those exploited or coerced into carrying drugs typically receive significant downward
departures.
Addiction context - relevant but not in itself sufficient for departure; relevant to broader offender-circumstances
analysis.
Aggravation - very large quantities, weapons in connection with drugs, organised crime context, supply to
vulnerable victims (minors, persons in addiction) - all aggravate.
Practitioners should monitor the Courts Service judgments database for ongoing developments.

Part C - Possession and Intent Jurisprudence
C.1 The Concept of Possession in Irish Drug Law
Possession is a foundational concept across the MDA. The case law has clarified:
Physical custody - the person has the drug on or about them.
Constructive possession - the person has control of the place / container where the drug is, and knows of its
presence.
Joint possession - multiple persons can have possession of the same drug where each knows and consents to
the joint control.
Brief / fleeting possession - momentary handling without knowledge may not amount to possession (e.g., person
hands an item to be passed on; the intermediate person may not have possession in the technical sense).

C.2 The Knowledge Element
Possession requires knowledge that the substance is present and is a controlled drug. The case law:
Knowledge of the substance's presence - inferred from circumstances (location of find, proximity, suspect's
reaction).
Knowledge that the substance is a controlled drug - the accused need not know the specific drug or class but
must know it is a controlled substance.
Reasonable doubt as to knowledge defeats the charge - hence the importance of evidence on the suspect's
knowledge.

C.3 Intent to Supply - Inference
Intent to supply under s.15 is inferred from circumstantial evidence:
Quantity beyond personal use - the principal indicator.
Packaging - multiple individual deals, scales, bags.
Paraphernalia of supply - cutting agents, weighing equipment.
Cash inconsistent with declared income.
Multiple phones - particularly 'burner' phones.
Observed transactions.
Communications evidence.
The case law confirms that the jury (or judge in summary cases) infers intent from the totality of the
circumstances. Expert / experienced Garda evidence on what these indicators mean in current drug market
practice is admissible and operationally important.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part D - Evidence Authorities Specific to Drug Investigation
D.1 Chain of Custody in Drug Cases
The chain of custody from seizure to court production is the foundation of drug prosecution. The case law
confirms:
Each handling of the exhibit must be documented - who, when, why.
Continuity must be established without gap.
Tamper-evident sealing and labelling.
Storage in secure conditions.
FSI submission and return chain documented.
Challenges to chain of custody are common in drug cases - some are technically demanding. The investigation
must be rigorous from the moment of seizure.

D.2 FSI Reports - Status and Admissibility
Forensic Science Ireland reports are admitted as expert evidence. The forensic chemist who analysed the
substance gives evidence at trial (or the report is admitted by consent in many cases). The report establishes:
Identity of the substance (chemical analysis).
Weight (typically with reference to packaging).
Purity (in cases where purity affects market value or sentencing).
Sometimes - origin / batch characteristics.

D.3 Market Value Evidence
Market value evidence for s.15A threshold purposes is given by trained Garda witnesses (typically from
specialist drugs units). The Court of Criminal Appeal has confirmed the admissibility of this evidence as expert
/ opinion evidence based on training and market knowledge. The valuation evidence is subject to crossexamination but is the standard mechanism for establishing the EUR 13,000 threshold.

D.4 Communications Data and Phone Evidence
Phone evidence is now central in drug investigations. The case law and statutory framework around
communications data have developed substantially. Operational considerations:
Statutory access framework (Communications Retention of Data Acts 2011, 2022) following CJEU
jurisprudence.
Forensic phone examination procedures.
Evidence admission - the forensic examiner gives evidence on extraction and findings.

Part E - Sentencing Authority Applied to Drugs
E.1 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250
Operational Application (Drugs): Structured discretion framework applies. Drug-specific aggravators: scale;
role; vulnerable victim (minor, person in addiction, prisoner); organised crime context; weapons in connection;
international dimension; cumulative impact. Drug-specific mitigators: plea (early); cooperation (material);
addiction context (offender-side); courier role (limited principal involvement); first offence; family
circumstances; rehabilitation prospects.

E.2 The People (DPP) v M [1994] 3 IR 306
Operational Application (Drugs): Refines and applies the Tiernan framework to drug sentencing. The
structured analysis is the consistent approach.

Part F - Constitutional and Convention Dimensions in Drug Investigation
F.1 Article 40.5 - Inviolability of the Dwelling (Damache)
Drug search warrants for dwellings require independent authorisation. The Damache framework operates.

F.2 Article 38.1 - Fair Trial
Procedural integrity in drug investigation (custody, interview, evidence handling) feeds the fair trial guarantee.
Procedural breaches risk evidence exclusion (JC) and may engage the wider fair trial dimension.

F.3 Article 8 ECHR - Privacy
Stop and search, premises search, phone examination, communications data access all engage Article 8.
Restrictions must be prescribed by law, pursue a legitimate aim, and be proportionate. The Irish framework
operates within this.

F.4 ECtHR on Drug Investigation - Selected Authority
ECtHR has considered drug investigation across multiple cases. Key themes:
Article 6 fair trial - undercover and surveillance evidence must be obtained within proper limits.
Article 3 - police force in drug operations must be proportionate (Cestaro line).
Article 5 - detention must be lawful and proportionate.
Article 8 - privacy intrusions must be necessary and proportionate.

Part G - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
G.1 Procedural Rigour
Drug cases are routinely contested on procedural grounds. Comply rigorously with arrest, search, custody,
interview, exhibit, and warrant frameworks. The JC margin is narrow.

G.2 The s.15A Presumption is Real but Not Mandatory
The 10-year presumption applies but is not automatic. The structured exception analysis allows for substantial
departures where the case justifies. The file build supports informed analysis.

G.3 Document the Role
Sentencing turns substantially on the offender's role. Build the file to capture role specifically - courier / holder
/ mid-level / principal / organiser / facilitator.

G.4 Build the Knowledge / Intent Picture
Knowledge and intent are inferred from circumstances. The file should marshal the indicators specifically quantity, packaging, cash, phones, communications, observation.

G.5 Chain of Custody
The chain from seizure to court is the case foundation. Rigorous documentation at every handling step.

G.6 The Sentencing File Inputs
Antecedent statement; Victim Impact Statement (where applicable - e.g., s.15B prison context); detailed
offender role analysis; cooperation record; plea history. All inform the Tiernan / s.15A sentencing exercise.

Volume 6 - A Note on the Material
This volume addresses sexual offences in operational detail because Garda members will encounter these
incidents and must be equipped to respond. The material is treated with the professional gravity it deserves.
Sexual offences cause profound and lasting harm to victims. The Garda response - first contact, investigation,
prosecution support - is itself part of the recovery framework. Get it right matters more here than perhaps
anywhere else in the criminal-justice spectrum.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How to Use Volume 6
Volume 6 covers sexual offences policing. It is structured around the operational reality that sexual offences
require a victim-centred, trauma-informed approach combined with technically rigorous investigation:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Victim-Centred Framework
Sexual offences investigations differ from most criminal investigations in a critical respect: the victim is the
principal evidence. Investigation cannot succeed without the victim's continued engagement, and the victim's
recovery is profoundly affected by how the investigation is conducted. The framework rests on four
foundations:

Foundation 1 - Belief
The starting point is to believe the victim. This does not mean uncritical acceptance of every detail - the
investigation will test and verify - but the operational posture is one of belief, not scepticism. Disbelief signals
communicate strongly to victims and are a recognised driver of withdrawal from the criminal justice system.

Foundation 2 - Control
The victim has had control taken from them. The investigation must restore agency wherever possible. This
means consultation on pace, venue, who is present, what information is sought, what investigative steps are
taken. The victim is not an evidential object but a person whose participation is voluntary.

Foundation 3 - Trauma-Informed Practice
Trauma affects memory, presentation, narrative, demeanour. Trauma-informed practice recognises:
Memory may be fragmented, non-linear, with peripheral detail more vivid than central facts.
Demeanour varies widely - flat affect, distress, calm, distance - none of which is itself evidence of credibility.
Disclosure is rarely complete in one session - layered disclosure is normal.
Inconsistencies between accounts can reflect trauma, not fabrication.
Physical responses (tonic immobility, freezing) can prevent resistance - their absence does not negate nonconsent.

Foundation 4 - Procedural Rigour
Sexual offences cases are contested intensely at trial. Procedural integrity is the foundation of admissibility.
Caution; pre-interview consultation; chain of custody; SATU evidence handling; statement-taking compliance
- every step matters. There is no tension between victim-centred and procedurally rigorous: both serve the same
goal of a sustainable case.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Forensic Urgency
Sexual offences generate biological and trace evidence that degrades over time. The Locard exchange principle
applies - contact transfers material in both directions. Operational implications:
SATU referral as early as practicable - ideally within 72 hours of the incident, though SATU accepts referrals
beyond.
Scene preservation - clothing, bedding, surfaces - bagged and labelled, dry-stored.
Victim advised to avoid washing, eating, drinking, urinating where possible until SATU examination (subject
to victim welfare priority).
Suspect arrest and SATU-style examination (where consented to / lawfully required) within the early window.
Clothing and items collected from victim and suspect for forensic analysis.
Forensic Science Ireland - biological / DNA examination.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

SATU - The Sexual Assault Treatment Unit
SATUs are HSE clinical units providing forensic medical examination and care for victims of sexual assault.
Six SATUs operate across Ireland (Dublin, Cork, Galway, Letterkenny, Mullingar, Waterford). The Garda
interface:
Referral - Gardai refer victims to SATU. The victim may also self-refer.
Examination - SATU clinician conducts forensic medical examination, providing both clinical care and
evidential collection.
Sexual Offences Examination Kit (SOEK) - the standardised evidence kit used by SATU.
Photography of injuries by SATU.
Documentation - SATU medical and forensic report becomes evidence.
Continued clinical care - STI screening, emergency contraception, mental health support, follow-up.
Victim need not have decided to report formally to engage SATU - examination may occur on a 'sample stored'
basis where the victim has not decided whether to report.

Verification reminder
Sexual offences law has been substantially reformed in recent decades (CL(R)(A) Act 1990, CL(SO)A 1993,
CL(SO)A 2006, CL(SO)A 2017). The 2017 Act consolidated consent doctrine and expanded child sexual
offences. Garda procedures and Tusla protocols continue to develop. Verify the current statutory framework,
Garda HQ directives, and Tusla protocols before relying.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Sexual Offences Application
Sexual offences policing engages the Code of Ethics at heightened intensity. The Garda member is dealing with
persons in profound vulnerability; the State's response shapes the victim's recovery; the prosecution outcome
turns on the integrity of the Garda case-build. Six Standards apply with particular significance.

Standard 1 - Duty
The duty to protect life includes the duty to investigate sexual offences effectively. The State has positive
obligations under Article 3 ECHR (prohibition of inhuman or degrading treatment) and Article 8 (private and
family life) to investigate sexual offences with diligence. M.C. v Bulgaria (2003) establishes the operational
dimension of this obligation - particularly in respect of consent-based investigations.

Standard 2 - Honesty and Integrity
Sexual offences investigations are contested intensely. The Garda statement-taking, evidence handling, and
procedural compliance must withstand sustained adversarial scrutiny. Honesty in recording what was said,
observed, and done is the foundation.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Force in sexual offences scenes is rarely required - the victim is not the subject of force, and suspect arrests are
typically conducted with appropriate preparation. Where force is used (e.g., to effect arrest of a violent suspect),
the standard analysis applies - lawful, necessary, proportionate.

Standard 5 - Respect for Others
Sexual offences victims are persons in profound distress and vulnerability. The Garda demeanour, language,
and process all communicate respect or its absence. Patience, dignity, validation, calm professional engagement
- these are not soft skills but operational requirements. Secondary victimisation - the further harm caused by
insensitive handling - is itself a recognised harm and an investigative failure.

Standard 6 - Impartiality
Sexual offences victims come from across the demographic spectrum. The Garda response must be impartial the same engagement standard whether the victim is male, female, non-binary; whether young, middle-aged,
elderly; whether sober or intoxicated at the time of the offence; whether the victim is a sex worker; whether the
victim has a prior relationship with the suspect; whether the victim has prior reports. The threshold of
investigative response should not vary by victim characteristics.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
Sexual offences investigations sometimes encounter culture - within Garda environments, in community
responses, in trial defence strategies - that minimises or normalises the offending. Where colleagues minimise
("she changed her mind"; "she was drunk"; "sure they were going out"), the duty to speak up applies.

The Six Sexual Offences Ethics Trip-Wires

The Vulnerability Dimension
Sexual offences victims may have specific vulnerabilities: children, adults with disabilities, victims with mental
health conditions, victims under the influence of substances at the time, victims in coercive relationships, sex
workers, immigrants without status. Vulnerability is not a reason to discount the offence - it often explains why
the offence was committed against this particular victim. Operational engagement adjusts to vulnerability:
Specialist interviewers (Garda Specialist Victim Interviewers).
Intermediaries where communication needs require.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Multi-agency support engagement.
Pace adjusted to victim capacity and welfare.
Continued engagement through court process.

Compliments - Sexual Offences Categories
Strong first response - calm, validating, evidence-preserving, victim-supportive.
Sustained investigative engagement - cases that take months or years to build.
Effective specialist interview - trauma-informed, comprehensive, evidentially strong.
Historical CSA case progress - complex disclosure investigations.
Multi-agency coordination - SATU, Tusla, victim services aligned with criminal investigation.
Victim support through trial - the Garda role does not end at file submission.

The Trauma Impact on Investigators
Sexual offences work carries significant occupational psychological cost. Cumulative exposure to traumatic
accounts, distressing material (especially CSAM), and high-stakes investigations affects Garda welfare. Use
Garda welfare services. Speak to supervisors and DVLO / DSCB colleagues. Specialist sexual offences
investigators have particular welfare support frameworks. Acknowledging the cost is professional, not
weakness.
